svg.title: MIPS CPU memory hierarchy and cache system diagram
svg.desc: Shows IMEM, cache bypass logic, three cache types (direct-mapped, set-associative, fully-associative), DMEM with latency, and how mem_stall feeds back to the CPU.

g.onclick: sendPrompt('What memory signals does the CPU expose to the memory system?')
sendPrompt('Explain the instruction memory (imem) — size, addressing, and how it connects to the CPU')
sendPrompt('Explain the cache_en bypass logic — how does the computer route directly to DMEM when cache is disabled?')
sendPrompt('Explain the direct-mapped cache: 16 blocks, index bits, tag bits, hit/miss FSM')
sendPrompt('Explain the set-associative cache: how many sets and ways, replacement policy')
sendPrompt('Explain the fully-associative cache used in computer.sv — how many entries, LRU replacement, IDLE/FETCHING FSM')
sendPrompt('Walk through the cache FSM: what happens on a miss, when does mem_stall go high?')
sendPrompt('What is the FETCHING state in the cache FSM and when does mem_stall deassert?')
sendPrompt('Explain the DMEM: latency counter (LATENCY=10 cycles), dmem_ready, word-aligned addressing')
sendPrompt('How does the DMEM latency counter work — how many cycles before dmem_ready asserts?')

svg.text: Address layout (direct-mapped example): [31:6] tag (26-bit) [5:2] index (4-bit → 16 sets) [1:0] byte offset (ignored, word-aligned)
